did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model to automatically diagnose and classify skin cancer. The specific architecture included convolutional layers, MaxPooling layers, Dropout layers, Flatten layers, and Dense layers, optimized with Adam and sparse categorical cross-entropy. The model achieved a training accuracy of 95.56% and a validation accuracy of 90.95% in diagnosing different kinds of skin diseases (including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) using dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for skin cancer diagnosis. It details specific methods including data preprocessing, model architecture (convolutional, MaxPooling, Dropout, Flatten, Dense layers), and optimization (Adam optimizer). It reports original results such as training accuracy (95.56%), validation accuracy (90.95%), and loss values, and describes the implementation of a web application with a chatbot and speech synthesis. The context identifies it as a paper from the '3rd International Conference on Electronic Communication and Artificial Intelligence (IWECAI)'.

title: Skin Cancer Diagnosis and Medical Service System Based on Deep Learning Models
journal: 2022 3rd International Conference on Electronic Communication and Artificial Intelligence (IWECAI)
brief_summary: This study developed a skin cancer diagnosis and medical service system using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to automatically classify nine categories of skin diseases from dermoscopy images. The model architecture incorporated convolutional, MaxPooling, Dropout, Flatten, and Dense layers, optimized with the Adam optimizer and sparse categorical cross-entropy loss. Data was sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) database, consisting of 2,239 images split into 80% for training and 20% for validation. The CNN achieved a training accuracy of 95.56% and a validation accuracy of 90.95%, effectively addressing overfitting through data augmentation and dropout techniques. Additionally, the researchers implemented a web application featuring a chatbot and speech synthesis to provide diagnostic references for doctors and information for patients.

all_task_types.target: Nine categories of skin diseases (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion, Pigmented Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify dermoscopy images into nine discrete diagnostic categories. The model was trained using sparse categorical cross-entropy loss, achieving a training accuracy of 95.56% and a validation accuracy of 90.95%. The task is explicitly described as classifying skin cancer and diagnosing different kinds of skin diseases.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Database
datasets_used.description: A public academic and industrial cooperation database providing images for the diagnosis of melanoma and other skin diseases. The dataset was imbalanced, with different classes having different numbers of images. Data augmentation (rotation, mirroring, greyscale conversion) and normalization (resizing to 150x150 pixels, standardization of RGB values to [0,1]) were applied.

use.training: 80% of the dataset was used for training the CNN model.
use.validation: 20% of the dataset was used for validation (tuning hyperparameters).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results were reported as training and validation accuracy and loss values.

ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.notes: The dataset consisted of 2,239 images across 9 classes. It was split into 80% for training and 20% for validation. Data augmentation (rotation, mirroring, greyscale conversion) and normalization (resizing to 150x150 pixels, standardization to [0,1]) were applied to address class imbalance and overfitting. The model architecture included Conv2D, MaxPooling2D, Dropout, Flatten, and Dense layers, optimized with Adam and sparse categorical cross-entropy.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: rotating images (at most 8 degrees left or right), mirroring images, and converting images to greyscale to address class imbalance and prevent overfitting.
Image resizing: Normalization was used to resize all images to 150x150 pixels because the original dataset images did not have the same size.
Pixel value standardization: RGB channel values (range 0-255) were normalized to the range [0,1] to be easily accepted by the CNN.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated to the left or right by at most 8 degrees. The probability of performing this operation was 0.8.
Images were mirrored as part of the data augmentation process to address class imbalance and prevent overfitting.
Images were converted to greyscale as one of the tried operations for data augmentation.
3000 images were added to each class to address the imbalance in the dataset where some classes had many images while others had few.

conclusions: The proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model effectively diagnoses different kinds of skin diseases, achieving a training accuracy of 95.56% and a validation accuracy of 90.95%.
The model addresses the overfitting problem common in studies with few classes by utilizing data augmentation (rotation, mirroring, greyscale conversion) and dropout layers.
The system provides a practical medical service application that includes a website, a chatbot for patient communication, and a speech synthesis engine to broadcast detection results.

The application serves as a reference for doctors and provides convenience for patients, but the authors explicitly state that the predicted result is not equal to the final clinical diagnosis and should only be used as a reference.

The study highlights that many previous skin cancer studies ignored the importance of the application side, whereas this work combines deep learning prediction with a designed algorithm to bring real value to human life.
important_remarks: The study addresses class imbalance by synthetically adding 3,000 images to each class, which may introduce bias or unrealistic data distribution compared to natural prevalence.
The dataset is imbalanced with varying numbers of images per class, as shown in Figure 2, which the authors attempted to mitigate via augmentation and synthetic data.
The model was trained on 80% of the ISIC dataset and validated on 20%, but no independent external test set was used, limiting the assessment of generalizability.

The paper explicitly states that the predicted results are not equal to the final clinical diagnosis and should only be used as a reference for doctors, highlighting a critical limitation in clinical applicability.

The system includes a web application with a chatbot and speech synthesis, but the clinical utility is constrained by the lack of rigorous external validation and the disclaimer that it is not a diagnostic tool.

The study focuses on nine categories of skin diseases, including both malignant (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma) and benign conditions, but the high accuracy (90.95% validation) may be inflated due to the synthetic data augmentation strategy.
The authors note that previous studies often ignored the application side; this work combines deep learning with a user interface, but the clinical workflow integration is not evaluated.
